task: I’m about to hand off a finished edit and want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious before I send it. Can you help me build a quick last-pass checklist for picture, sound, and exports that’s realistic for a small team and not too bloated?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical last-pass checklist you can run right before handoff. Kept lean, but it should catch the usual problems.

## Final handoff checklist

### 1) Picture
- Watch the full cut once, start to finish
- Check for:
  - black frames / flash frames
  - frozen frames
  - accidental gaps or jumps
  - wrong shots / temp shots still in place
  - missing slates, slugs, or end cards
- Confirm:
  - titles and lower-thirds are spelled correctly
  - any on-screen text matches the latest notes
  - aspect ratio / framing is correct
  - no stray guides, safe margins, or overlays are visible
- If relevant:
  - verify frame rate matches the delivery spec
  - check any speed ramps or retimes for weird motion or audio issues

### 2) Sound
- Listen through the full piece, at least once with headphones
- Check for:
  - clipped dialogue
  - obvious pops, clicks, hum, or dropouts
  - temp music or temp SFX still in the cut
  - missing ambience or dead air where it shouldn’t be
  - music/dialogue balance that feels off
- Confirm:
  - fades are clean
  - end music doesn’t cut off early
  - any mix notes from the client are reflected
- If there’s a mix deliverable:
  - make sure stems are named correctly and line up

### 3) Exports
- Confirm export settings match the delivery spec
  - codec
  - resolution
  - frame rate
  - audio format / sample rate
- Open the exported file and spot-check:
  - start
  - middle
  - end
- Make sure the export includes:
  - correct version name
  - correct date or revision tag, if you use one
  - no “test,” “temp,” or “final_final” language
- Verify file plays all the way through without corruption
- Check file size is reasonable for the format

### 4) Package check
- Delivery folder contains only the intended files
- No duplicates unless they’re supposed to be there
- Readme / notes file is current, if you include one
- Reference stills / captions / transcripts are included if requested
- File names are consistent and easy to understand

### 5) Final sanity check
- Does this match the latest client notes?
- Are you handing off the approved version, not an earlier export?
- If anything changed late, is it documented clearly?

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter one-page checklist you can actually paste into a project doc or use as a printout.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good. Not bloated, which is the main thing. But I’d tighten it a bit more for actual handoff use — less generic, more stuff that catches the dumb expensive mistakes.

I’d want one more pass on these:
- embedded captions / subtitles if they’re part of delivery
- legal / music cue sheet stuff if applicable
- leader / tail slate / handles if the client asked for them
- audio channel layout, not just ‘sounds okay’ — especially if it’s stereo vs 5.1 or split stems
- loudness target if it’s broadcast or web spec driven
- naming consistency across export, folder, and upload portal

Also, the export section should call out:
- open the file in a different player if possible, not just the NLE
- check first and last 10 seconds, not just start/middle/end
- confirm there isn’t any accidental offset, black at head, or truncated tail

If you want, I’d rather have this in a checkbox format I can use fast, like:
- picture
- audio
- delivery
- package
- spec check

Short, no fluff. Something I can run in 10 minutes when the client is already waiting.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, fast-run handoff checklist in the order you’d actually use it.

## 10-minute final handoff checklist

### Picture
- [ ] Watch the cut once, start to finish
- [ ] Check for flash frames, black frames, frozen frames, or accidental gaps
- [ ] Confirm no temp shots, placeholder graphics, or wrong versions are still in
- [ ] Check titles / lower-thirds / on-screen text for spelling and latest notes
- [ ] Verify framing, aspect ratio, and safe areas are clean
- [ ] If relevant: confirm frame rate, retimes, and mixed media don’t cause weird motion

### Audio
- [ ] Listen through once with headphones
- [ ] Check for pops, clicks, hum, dropouts, clipped dialogue, or dead air
- [ ] Confirm temp music / temp SFX are gone if they shouldn’t be there
- [ ] Check fades, music endings, and any transition points
- [ ] Verify audio channel layout matches delivery:
  - [ ] stereo
  - [ ] 5.1
  - [ ] split stems
  - [ ] other: ________
- [ ] If applicable, confirm loudness target is met for spec
- [ ] If applicable, confirm embedded captions / subtitles are present and correct

### Delivery / export
- [ ] Export settings match spec:
  - [ ] codec
  - [ ] resolution
  - [ ] frame rate
  - [ ] audio format / sample rate
- [ ] File name matches the agreed naming format
- [ ] Version / date / rev tag is consistent across export, folder, and upload portal
- [ ] Open the exported file in a different player if possible
- [ ] Check first 10 seconds
- [ ] Check last 10 seconds
- [ ] Confirm no black at head, accidental offset, or truncated tail
- [ ] Spot-check somewhere in the middle too
- [ ] Make sure the file plays cleanly all the way through

### Package
- [ ] Delivery folder contains only the intended files
- [ ] No duplicate or old exports left in the handoff folder
- [ ] Captions / subtitles included if required
- [ ] Cue sheet / legal / music paperwork included if applicable
- [ ] Leader / tail slate / handles included if requested
- [ ] Notes / readme is current, if you use one
- [ ] Naming is consistent across all files

### Final spec check
- [ ] Matches the latest client notes
- [ ] Approved version is the one being sent
- [ ] Any late changes are documented
- [ ] Nothing in the folder is “temp,” “test,” or stale

If you want, I can turn this into a super-clean one-page version with just checkboxes and no explanation text.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Tight enough to use, but it still catches the stuff that actually costs you.

I’d use that as-is, maybe just minor wording cleanup if I was pasting it into a template. The added bits — captions, cue sheet, handles, channel layout, loudness, naming consistency — that’s the real handoff risk stuff.

No need to overbuild it.
